did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble of ten artificial neural networks (ANNs) combined with a final decision-making neural network to classify malignant melanoma from dermatoscopic images. The system utilizes hand-crafted features based on the extended ABCD rule (Asymmetry, Border, Colour, Differential structural components) extracted via image processing, which are then fed into the neural network ensemble for classification. The results (AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) were obtained using this AI-based diagnostic system.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a new intelligent decision support system based on an ensemble of ten neural networks for diagnosing malignant melanoma. It details the methodology, including an extended ABCD rule for hand-crafted feature extraction, image pre-processing, and the specific neural network architecture. The authors present original experimental results, stating the system was trained on over 5000 samples and tested on 200 skin moles, aiming to demonstrate improved efficiency compared to single neural networks. This constitutes an original technical study producing new results.

title: DIAGNOSIS OF MALIGNANT MELANOMA BY NEURAL NETWORK ENSEMBLE-BASED SYSTEM UTILISING HAND-CRAFTED SKIN LESION FEATURES
brief_summary: This study proposes an intelligent decision support system for diagnosing malignant melanoma using an ensemble of ten artificial neural networks combined with a final decision-making network. The system utilizes hand-crafted features derived from an extended ABCD rule applied to dermatoscopic images, including asymmetry, border, color, and structural components. The model was trained on a dataset of 5,735 skin moles (4,759 benign and 976 malignant) and evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation on 200 test images. The ensemble approach significantly improved performance compared to single neural networks, achieving average metrics of 84.92% AUC, 84.40% accuracy, 90.80% sensitivity, and 78.00% specificity. The authors conclude that this transparent, feature-based system offers competitive results to deep learning methods while providing greater interpretability for medical specialists.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary diagnostic target of the study. The system was trained on 976 malignant moles and tested on 100 malignant moles. Output values > 0.5 indicate a malignant lesion.
The negative class for the binary classification task. The system was trained on 4,759 benign moles and tested on 100 benign moles. Output values <= 0.5 indicate a benign lesion.

all_task_types.target: Malignant melanoma vs. Benign skin lesions
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task to diagnose malignant melanoma from dermatoscopic images. The system classifies lesions as malignant (output > 0.5) or benign (output <= 0.5). Performance metrics include AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of dermatoscopic images of skin moles used for training and testing the proposed neural network ensemble system. The specific source or collection method is not detailed in the text, but it is distinct from the public datasets mentioned in the introduction (Dermofit, MoleMap, ISIC, ISBI).

use.training: Used to train the ensemble of ten artificial neural networks and the final decision-making network. The dataset was split into five representative sets for 5-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used via 5-fold cross-validation procedure to investigate generalization ability. The dataset was divided into five different representative sets of images.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the system's performance (AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) across the 5 testing folds.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the generalization ability of the neural network ensemble system and obtain performance metrics (AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) on the test set.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) and 5-fold cross-validation.

Custom Dermoscopic Image Dataset (5-fold.description: Results for single neural networks (averaged over 10 networks) and the final ensemble decision support system across 5 testing folds.
Custom Dermoscopic Image Dataset (5-fold.notes: The ensemble approach increased average AUC by 6.02%, accuracy by 6.72%, sensitivity by 9.80%, and specificity by 3.64% compared to single neural networks. Individual fold results for the ensemble were: Fold 1 (AUC: 80.02, Acc: 79.50, Sens: 80.00, Spec: 79.00), Fold 2 (AUC: 85.53, Acc: 85.00, Sens: 94.00, Spec: 76.00), Fold 3 (AUC: 85.49, Acc: 86.00, Sens: 94.00, Spec: 78.00), Fold 4 (AUC: 85.53, Acc: 85.00, Sens: 94.00, Spec: 76.00), Fold 5 (AUC: 88.02, Acc: 86.50, Sens: 92.00, Spec: 81.00).

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of ten feedforward artificial neural networks (ANNs) combined with a final decision-making feedforward ANN
architecture_used.input_size: 14 hand-crafted features (A, B, C3-C6, D1-D5) after excluding asymmetry (A) and red-dominating color (C2)

architecture_details.ensemble_structure: 10 neurons in hidden layer with logsig (sigmoid) activation function; 1 output linear neuron
architecture_details.final_decision_network: 1 feedforward neural network with 10 neurons in hidden layer and 1 output neuron; all neurons use logsig (sigmoid) activation function

training_details.initialization: Different initial values of weights and biases for each network in the ensemble

classification_or_output_head.threshold: Output values > 0.5 indicate malignant; <= 0.5 indicate benign
classification_or_output_head.final_aggregation: Voting system from ensemble outputs fed into final decision network

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble of ten feedforward artificial neural networks (ANNs)
Final decision-making feedforward artificial neural network
Single neural networks (individual members of the ensemble)
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) (mentioned as baseline/comparison in literature review)
preprocessing_techniques: Removal of black frames around the picture
Hair detection and reduction using corner detection filters (convolution matrix rotated 0 and 180 degrees) on RGB layers, binary summation, and deletion of small elements to create a hair mask
Hair replacement using morphological closing for image estimation and pixel replacement from a blurred image
Reduction of the number of colors

Automated segmentation of skin moles using two complementary methods: Method I (erosion and thresholding) and Method II (RGB channel arithmetic operations R+B-G and G-R-B, contrast enhancement, color reversal, and thresholding)
Final segmentation mask refinement (removal of small objects, filling holes, smoothing edges)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not report the use of data augmentation techniques (such as geometric transforms, color transforms, GANs, or synthetic data generation) during training. The study utilized hand-crafted features extracted from a fixed dataset of 5,735 skin moles (4,759 benign, 976 malignant) and evaluated the model using 5-fold cross-validation.

conclusions: The proposed neural network ensemble-based decision support system significantly improves diagnostic efficiency compared to single neural networks, increasing average AUC by 6.02%, accuracy by 6.72%, sensitivity by 9.80%, and specificity by 3.64%.
The ensemble system achieved an average performance of 84.92% AUC, 84.40% accuracy, 90.80% sensitivity, and 78.00% specificity on a test set of 200 skin moles.

Excluding the asymmetry (A) and red-dominating color (C2) features from the input set improved the final AUC and accuracy scores, indicating these features were detrimental to the model's performance.

The system utilizes hand-crafted features based on an extended ABCD rule, offering a transparent decision-making process that allows medical specialists to oversee the diagnosis, contrasting with the 'black box' nature of deep learning methods.
The system is suitable for use as a decision support tool for primary care physicians, for self-examination of the skin with a dermatoscope, and as a tool to improve biopsy decision-making.

The study demonstrates that an ensemble of ten parallel feedforward neural networks combined with a final decision-making network is an effective approach for classifying malignant melanoma from dermatoscopic images.
important_remarks: The study uses a private, custom dataset (5,735 training, 200 testing images) rather than public benchmarks like ISIC or Dermofit, limiting external validation and generalizability.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; results are reported as descriptive metrics from 5-fold cross-validation.
The dataset is significantly imbalanced, with 4,759 benign moles versus only 976 malignant moles in the training set.

The paper acknowledges that hand-crafted feature extraction is difficult due to image artifacts (hair, markers, uneven quality) and relies on complex pre-processing steps that may not be robust across different clinical settings.

The authors explicitly state that pre-processing and segmentation steps are not the primary contribution and can be replaced by other methods, suggesting the feature extraction pipeline is not fully standardized or optimized.
Performance claims are based on a small test set (200 images total, 100 per class), which may lead to high variance in performance estimates.

The system excludes asymmetry (A) and red-dominating color (C2) features because they lowered performance, which contradicts the standard ABCD rule and may limit clinical interpretability for specialists relying on those specific criteria.

The paper compares its results qualitatively to deep learning methods but does not provide a direct quantitative comparison on the same dataset, making the claim of 'competitive' results difficult to verify.
The system is designed as a decision support tool for primary care physicians and self-examination, but no clinical workflow integration or user study was reported to validate its practical utility.

The authors note that deep learning methods can learn 'false features' (e.g., associating hair with malignancy), implying their hand-crafted approach is more robust to such artifacts, but this is not empirically tested against adversarial examples or out-of-distribution data.
